title: JavaScript Data Types
description: Explore JavaScript's primitive and reference data types. Learn how to work with strings, numbers, booleans, and more.
longDescription: JavaScript is dynamically typed, meaning variables can hold any type of value. In this lesson, you'll discover the different data types available in JavaScript and learn when to use each one.

learningObjectives.statement: Work with numbers and understand NaN

missionNarrative.title: The Data Type Garden
missionNarrative.story: Beyond the Variable Vault lies a beautiful garden where different types of data bloom like flowers. Each type has its own unique properties. Your task is to learn them all!

sections.title: Primitive Data Types
sections.content:
JavaScript has **7 primitive data types** that form the foundation of all your programs:

1. **String** - Text values
2. **Number** - Numeric values
3. **Boolean** - True or false
4. **Null** - Intentional absence of value
5. **Undefined** - Variable declared but not assigned
6. **Symbol** - Unique identifiers (advanced)
7. **BigInt** - Very large integers

Let's explore each one!

sections.title: Creating Primitive Values
Pro Tip
sections.content:
Use the **typeof** operator to check a value's type:

```javascript
typeof "hello"  // "string"
typeof 42       // "number"
typeof true     // "boolean"
typeof null     // "object" (historical bug!)
typeof undefined // "undefined"
```

sections.title: Working with Strings
sections.content:
## String Methods

Strings come with many useful built-in methods:

```javascript
const text = "Hello, World!";

text.length           // 13
text.toUpperCase()    // "HELLO, WORLD!"
text.toLowerCase()    // "hello, world!"
text.includes("World") // true
text.slice(0, 5)      // "Hello"
text.split(", ")       // ["Hello", "World!"]
```

## Template Literals

Use backticks (\`) for string interpolation:

```javascript
const name = "Bob";
const age = 30;
const message = `${name} is ${age} years old`;
// "Bob is 30 years old"
```

sections.title: Numbers in JavaScript
Watch Out!
sections.content:
**NaN** (Not a Number) is tricky:

```javascript
"hello" * 5  // NaN
NaN === NaN  // false! Use isNaN() instead
isNaN(NaN)   // true
```

NaN propagates through calculations, so catch errors early!

challenges.title: Identify the Data Type
challenges.description: Test your knowledge of JavaScript data types
challenges.question: What is the data type of each value?
challenges.instructions: Match each value with its correct data type.

options.explanation: Incorrect! '42' is a string because it's in quotes.
Tricky! typeof null returns 'object' due to a historical bug.

challenges.successFeedback: Excellent! You've mastered JavaScript data types!
challenges.failureFeedback: Keep practicing! Remember that values in quotes are strings.

hints.text: Values wrapped in quotes are always strings.
true and false are the only boolean values.
typeof null returning 'object' is a famous JavaScript quirk!

challenges.title: String Method Practice
challenges.description: Apply string methods to transform text
challenges.question: Complete the string transformations
challenges.instructions: Fill in the blanks to achieve the desired result.
challenges.successFeedback: Perfect! You're becoming a string manipulation expert!
challenges.failureFeedback: Review the string methods section and try again.

hints.text: Method names in JavaScript use camelCase.
length is a property, not a method - no parentheses needed!

reflectionQuestion.question: Which data type do you think you'll use most often in your programs? Why?
reflectionQuestion.sampleAnswer: I think I'll use strings most often because they're essential for displaying messages, working with user input, and manipulating text. Numbers will be close second for calculations.
